definition: HyperText Transfer Protocol - the foundation of data communication on the World Wide Web.
The HTTP Host header specifies the domain name of the server to which the request is being sent.
A port is a numerical identifier in networking used to direct traffic to a specific service on a host.
HTTP headers are key-value pairs sent between client and server to convey metadata about the request or response.
The process of encoding data so that only authorised parties can read it; used in HTTPS via TLS.
An HTTP method used to request a representation of a resource without modifying it.
An HTTP method used to submit data to a server
column3: often causing a change in state or side effects.

definition: Uniform Resource Locator - the address used to locate a resource on the internet.
A three-digit number in an HTTP response indicating the result of the request (e.g. 200 OK or 404 Not Found).
Storing copies of responses so that future requests can be served faster without contacting the origin server.
An HTTP header indicating the media type of the resource (e.g. text/html or application/json).
Small pieces of data stored on the client by the server to maintain state across HTTP requests.
A server-side mechanism that preserves state between multiple HTTP requests from the same client using a session ID.
Representational State Transfer - an architectural style for designing networked applications using stateless HTTP requests.
JavaScript Object Notation - a lightweight text format for exchanging data between a server and a client.
eXtensible Markup Language - a markup language used to encode documents in a format that is both human-readable and machine-readable.
